jokes.text: Wait, how could Liz win a fellowship award? She doesn't like people. No, followship.
jokes.setup: Character questions how antisocial Liz could win a fellowship award
jokes.punchline: Correction reveals it's 'followship' - an award for being a follower
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay that perfectly sets up the episode's theme about Liz being a corporate follower. The misdirection is clean and the payoff is immediate.

jokes.text: Presented annually to the woman... sorry, person, who best exemplifies a follower.
jokes.setup: Jack announces the award formally
jokes.punchline: Self-corrects from 'woman' to 'person' in corporate PC fashion
jokes.explanation: Solid corporate satire that fits Jack's character perfectly. Good setup for his corporate doublespeak.

jokes.text: I'm not a follower. It also comes with ten grand. I accept this proudly on behalf of followers everywhere.
jokes.setup: Liz denies being a follower
jokes.punchline: Immediately accepts award when money is mentioned, proving she is indeed a follower
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Liz's hypocrisy and weakness for money. The instant 180 is beautifully executed.

jokes.text: So resistant to product integration, cross-promotion, and adverlingus.
jokes.setup: Jack lists corporate buzzwords Liz used to resist
jokes.punchline: Ends with the made-up word 'adverlingus' mixing advertising with cunnilingus
jokes.explanation: Brilliant made-up word that perfectly captures 30 Rock's corporate satire while being hilariously inappropriate.

jokes.text: So what are you gonna do with your money, put it into a 401K? Yeah, I gotta get one of those. What?
jokes.setup: Jack asks if Liz will invest responsibly in a 401K
jokes.punchline: Liz admits she doesn't even have basic retirement planning
jokes.explanation: Good character-based humor showing Liz's financial irresponsibility, sets up the episode's money theme.

jokes.text: Where do you invest your money, Liz? I have, like, 12 grand in checking. Are you an immigrant?
jokes.setup: Jack asks about Liz's investment strategy
jokes.punchline: Her primitive financial habits are so bad Jack thinks she must be an immigrant
jokes.explanation: Sharp escalation with Jack's characteristic elitism. The immigrant line is both offensive and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: If I can't be Monique fat, I have to be Teri hatcher thin. Either way, you're laughin'.
jokes.setup: Jenna explains her weight loss strategy
jokes.punchline: She only operates in extremes, both for audience amusement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jenna moment showing her narcissism and calculated approach to getting attention through her appearance.

jokes.text: Is she one of the ladies who tried to shoot Gerald Ford?
jokes.setup: Asked about comedy legend Rosemary Howard
jokes.punchline: Liz's only reference point is assassination attempts on presidents
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully specific and absurd reference that shows Liz's weird knowledge gaps. The Ford assassination attempts are a perfect obscure callback.

jokes.text: Pardon me! Pardon you? You were already pardoned! It's funny 'cause it's true.
jokes.setup: Rosemary says 'Pardon me'
jokes.punchline: Makes a Ford pardon reference then explains her own joke
jokes.explanation: Decent callback to the Gerald Ford reference but explaining the joke weakens it. Shows Rosemary as past her prime.

jokes.text: When I mean my friends, I mean my Fisher Price... My friend dolls. Because I didn't have a lot of friends.
jokes.setup: Liz talks about acting out skits with friends
jokes.punchline: Reveals she had to use dolls because she had no real friends
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment that reveals Liz's lonely childhood. The self-correction adds to the awkwardness.

jokes.text: Oh, boy. Am I still talking? You're gonna kill me, aren't you?
jokes.setup: Liz realizes she's been oversharing
jokes.punchline: Paranoid assumption that her hero will murder her for being annoying
jokes.explanation: Classic Liz anxiety spiral with characteristic paranoid escalation. Good character work.

jokes.text: I grew up wanting to be Samantha Stevens on bewitched. Closest I got was being married to a gay guy for two years.
jokes.setup: Rosemary shares her childhood dream of being a TV witch
jokes.punchline: Dark twist about her failed marriage to a closeted man
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect structure. The connection between magical powers and marital deception is clever.

jokes.text: And by heroine, I mean lady hero. I don't wanna inject you and listen to jazz.
jokes.setup: Liz calls Rosemary her heroine
jokes.punchline: Clarifies she means female hero, not the drug, with absurd drug stereotype
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay with an absurd drug reference. The jazz detail makes it even funnier and more specific.

jokes.text: If you desecrate something, is that bad?
jokes.setup: Tracy asks an innocent question about desecration
jokes.punchline: The fact that he doesn't know implies he's already done something terrible
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tracy moment showing his childlike ignorance paired with destructive behavior. Great economy.

jokes.text: Who would have ever known there was so many words? It was like a mos def cd.
jokes.setup: Tracy reflects on singing the national anthem
jokes.punchline: Compares patriotic song to conscious rap, showing his musical ignorance
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tracy's cultural disconnect and limited attention span. The Mos Def reference dates it but works.

jokes.text: Except no dog fighting, okay? That seems to be the one thing that's off limits these days.
jokes.setup: Jack tells Tracy he can do anything as a star
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions dog fighting is the one forbidden thing
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that perfectly sets up Tracy's contrarian nature. The casual delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Dog fighting it is. Make it happen.
jokes.setup: Kenneth tries to talk Tracy out of dog fighting
jokes.punchline: Tracy doubles down purely because he was told not to
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Tracy's commitment to bad decisions. The casual delivery sells it.

jokes.text: Man, this is Phil spector's entourage all over again.
jokes.setup: Kenneth faces an impossible moral situation with Tracy
jokes.punchline: References working for the murderous producer Phil Spector
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor suggesting Kenneth has worked for other problematic celebrities. The specificity makes it hilarious.

jokes.text: It's like sex, you know? It's almost better when everything goes horribly wrong.
jokes.setup: Rosemary compares live TV to sex
jokes.punchline: Reveals her preference for disaster in both contexts
jokes.explanation: Good character insight showing Rosemary's jaded perspective on both entertainment and intimacy.

jokes.text: Does everyone still do blow in Joe Garagiola's office? Which one is blow again? Is that cocaine?
jokes.setup: Rosemary asks about cocaine use at NBC
jokes.punchline: Liz's innocent confusion about drug terminology
jokes.explanation: Nice contrast between Rosemary's wild past and Liz's sheltered present. The Joe Garagiola detail is specific and weird.

jokes.text: Mr. Perfect, Mr. Jesus loves me Parcell finally messed up? Do you know how long I've been waiting to transfer that thumbhead to cnbc in paramus, New Jersey?
jokes.setup: Donnie learns Kenneth made a mistake
jokes.punchline: Reveals his elaborate plan to banish Kenneth to CNBC in New Jersey
jokes.explanation: Good setup of antagonist relationship. The specific location 'Paramus, New Jersey' makes the threat funnier.

jokes.text: You've probably never seen breasts before. So I'm gonna lean over this desk and you're gonna give me a new page jacket.
jokes.setup: Jenna assumes Kenneth is sexually inexperienced
jokes.punchline: Plans to use her sexuality to manipulate him into helping
jokes.explanation: Classic Jenna using her sexuality as currency. Good character moment showing her manipulative nature.

jokes.text: Please, I breast fed 'til I was 11. So I've forgotten more about a woman's chest than you'll ever know.
jokes.setup: Kenneth responds to Jenna's attempted seduction
jokes.punchline: Reveals disturbing backstory about extended breastfeeding
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor that completely subverts expectations. Perfect Kenneth moment - innocent delivery of deeply disturbing information.

jokes.text: But... I'm on TV. I said, 'good day.' No, you didn't. Well, I meant to.
jokes.setup: Jenna tries to make a dramatic exit
jokes.punchline: Kenneth corrects her failed dramatic moment, she weakly defends it
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Jenna's dramatic moment. Kenneth's literalness ruins her theatrical exit.

jokes.text: I have an idea. We open on a new Orleans abortion clinic. A beautiful mulatto... Uh, I don't think we're allowed to use any of those words.
jokes.setup: Rosemary pitches an edgy sketch idea
jokes.punchline: Immediately uses multiple forbidden words, Liz has to stop her
jokes.explanation: Good setup of Rosemary being out of touch with modern sensitivities. The rapid-fire offensive content is well-executed.

jokes.text: Don't you get it? The mailbox was haldeman. Is that a person who lived?
jokes.setup: Rosemary explains an old political reference
jokes.punchline: Liz doesn't even know if Haldeman was a real person
jokes.explanation: Good generational gap humor. Liz's complete ignorance of Watergate figures is believable and funny.

jokes.text: We get Josh in blackface, right? And then we get Tracy to call him a!
jokes.setup: Rosemary continues pitching edgy comedy
jokes.punchline: Suggests incredibly offensive blackface bit with racial slurs
jokes.explanation: Shows how out of touch Rosemary is with modern standards. Necessary for character but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: I'd watch that.
jokes.setup: Rosemary pitches offensive racial comedy
jokes.punchline: Tracy enthusiastically endorses the problematic content
jokes.explanation: Good Tracy moment showing his lack of social awareness. The brevity makes it funnier.

jokes.text: We would have done that on the Mandrell sisters!
jokes.setup: Defending her edgy comedy approach
jokes.punchline: Claims they would have done racial humor on a wholesome country variety show
jokes.explanation: Absurd reference that shows Rosemary's complete disconnect from reality. The Mandrell Sisters were notoriously wholesome.

jokes.text: Frank, what do you have this week? Uh, Barry the humping dog is shopping for a ge washer and dryer.
jokes.setup: Liz asks for subversive sketch ideas
jokes.punchline: Frank offers crude product placement disguised as edgy comedy
jokes.explanation: Good satire of how corporate demands corrupt creative rebellion. The contrast between 'edgy' and product placement is sharp.

jokes.text: What? No! You idiots! I built a dog fighting pit in my basement for this?
jokes.setup: Tracy sees the dogs Dot Com brought
jokes.punchline: Reveals he actually built infrastructure for dog fighting based on misunderstanding
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation showing Tracy's commitment to bad decisions. The basement pit detail makes it even more absurd.

jokes.text: Tracy, don't play with matches! You're not my dad! Tracy, don't stare directly at the sun. It'll make you crazy. You're not my dad.
jokes.setup: Jack tries to understand Tracy's oppositional behavior
jokes.punchline: Series of increasingly absurd examples of Tracy's defiance
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from reasonable advice to absurd warnings. The repetitive structure builds the comedy perfectly.

jokes.text: Hey, props gave me a bunch of dog penises for you to look at.
jokes.setup: Casual workplace interaction
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate props delivery said matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment showing how weird TV production can be. The casual delivery sells it.

jokes.text: Fire her. And don't ever make me talk to a woman that old again.
jokes.setup: Jack finishes pleasantries with Rosemary
jokes.punchline: Immediately reveals his complete disdain and ageism
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack moment showing his corporate ruthlessness and casual bigotry. The timing is excellent.

jokes.text: You know who uses that phrase? People who don't have the guts or the brains to work inside the system. Letter writers, radicals, Howard Dean.
jokes.setup: Jack mocks the phrase 'push the envelope'
jokes.punchline: Lists increasingly absurd examples ending with Howard Dean
jokes.explanation: Good Jack rant with perfect escalation. The Howard Dean reference was timely and specific to his reputation.

jokes.text: You got into this business because you're funny and you're weird and you're socially retarded.
jokes.setup: Jack explains why Liz really got into comedy
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest assessment of her social skills
jokes.explanation: Harsh but accurate character assessment from Jack. The directness makes it funnier.

jokes.text: What's a triangle graph? I don't know! It sounded real.
jokes.setup: Liz mentions triangle graphs in her rant
jokes.punchline: Admits she made up business terminology to sound smart
jokes.explanation: Great deflation of Liz's attempted sophisticated critique. Her honesty about making it up is perfect.

jokes.text: Suck it up, nerd.
jokes.setup: Liz gives passionate speech about artistic integrity
jokes.punchline: Jack's dismissive, juvenile response to her idealism
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Liz's grand stand. Jack's casual cruelty and perfect economy make this work.

jokes.text: Yes!
jokes.setup: Liz and Rosemary are told they're fired
jokes.punchline: Rosemary celebrates being fired as validation of their rebellious stance
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Rosemary's delusion about sticking it to the man. The enthusiasm is perfectly misplaced.

jokes.text: I don't need therapy. I'm just mentally ill.
jokes.setup: Jack suggests Tracy needs therapy
jokes.punchline: Tracy's illogical distinction between therapy and mental illness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Tracy logic that makes perfect sense in his world. The economy and absurdity are exceptional.

jokes.text: Tracy, what if I told you that the one thing you cannot do is therapy? You're not my dad! We're doing therapy!
jokes.setup: Jack uses reverse psychology on Tracy
jokes.punchline: Tracy immediately agrees to therapy when told he can't do it
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the established character pattern. Jack's manipulation is brilliant and Tracy's response is inevitable.

jokes.text: It's a savage contest. Mixing physical stamina with NBC trivia.
jokes.setup: Kenneth explains what a page-off is
jokes.punchline: Combines brutal physicality with pointless corporate knowledge
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist concept that perfectly satirizes corporate culture. The combination is hilariously specific.

jokes.text: Yeah, we can start our own network called bitch TV! Or the second idea that we think of.
jokes.setup: Rosemary excitedly proposes starting their own network
jokes.punchline: Immediately admits her first idea isn't very good
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware moment showing Rosemary's lack of filter and poor judgment. The immediate backtrack is funny.

jokes.text: Oh, they call it little chechnya. So gritty and real. More murders per capita than Detroit.
jokes.setup: Rosemary describes her neighborhood
jokes.punchline: Romanticizes an extremely dangerous area as 'authentic'
jokes.explanation: Good satire of artists romanticizing poverty and danger. The specific comparison to Detroit makes it sharper.

jokes.text: Is that guy carrying a gun? Yeah, but don't worry. He's not a cop.
jokes.setup: Liz notices someone with a gun
jokes.punchline: Rosemary's reassurance makes the situation worse, not better
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect misdirection. Rosemary's warped reassurance is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: Like my wife and I do? Cool. You be the maid. I want you to scream. Donaghy, you play the matador.
jokes.setup: Therapist suggests role play
jokes.punchline: Tracy thinks it's sexual roleplay and assigns bizarre roles
jokes.explanation: Good misunderstanding humor. Tracy's specific sexual scenario is appropriately weird and random.

jokes.text: Hey, dummy! I'm mad at you too. Why you gotta act out that way? Now, pass me them damn collard Greens!
jokes.setup: Jack tries to roleplay as Tracy's father
jokes.punchline: Immediately devolves into racial stereotypes
jokes.explanation: Shows Jack's cultural tone-deafness but relies on obvious stereotypes. Necessary setup for better jokes to follow.

jokes.text: Dyn-o-mite! I think we're just doing good times now.
jokes.setup: Jack continues his racial roleplay
jokes.punchline: Uses JJ Walker catchphrase; therapist notes they're just copying a sitcom
jokes.explanation: Good meta moment pointing out the absurdity. The therapist's clinical observation makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Lady, just because I'm an ignorant black man. And you paid me a nickel to bust up your chifforobe, doesn't give you the right to call me ridiculous just 'cause I'm proud of my son.
jokes.setup: Therapy session continues with Jack roleplaying
jokes.punchline: Jack delivers To Kill a Mockingbird reference as Tracy's father
jokes.explanation: Clever literary reference that shows Jack's education bleeding through. The 'chifforobe' detail is perfectly specific.

jokes.text: They got me. The honkeys shot me. No, dad! Don't die! I love you, dad!
jokes.setup: Roleplay reaches emotional climax
jokes.punchline: Jack dies dramatically; Tracy has genuine emotional breakthrough
jokes.explanation: Good escalation to an absurd but emotionally effective climax. The method is crazy but the result works.

jokes.text: You know, it's too bad you didn't know Howard Cosell when you were growing up. 'Cause I had that one in my pocket the whole time.
jokes.setup: Jack reflects on the successful therapy session
jokes.punchline: Reveals he could have done an even better impression
jokes.explanation: Good Jack smugness showing his preparation and random talents. Howard Cosell is a perfectly weird choice.

jokes.text: Women in their 50s join the army and get laid by a bunch of grateful 18 year olds. I predict opening weekend, a million dollars.
jokes.setup: Rosemary pitches her movie screenplay idea
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate premise with wildly unrealistic box office prediction
jokes.explanation: Shows Rosemary's complete disconnect from reality and commercial viability. The specific numbers make it funnier.

jokes.text: Oh, come on, Liz. It's the 90s!
jokes.setup: Rosemary defends her day drinking
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong about what decade it is
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of Rosemary being stuck in the past. The casual delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You pick up guys that are smart and funny. And they leave you for someone less complicated. You're never gonna get married, Liz.
jokes.setup: Rosemary says Liz is just like her
jokes.punchline: Brutal assessment of Liz's romantic future
jokes.explanation: Dark prophecy that hits too close to home for Liz. The specificity makes it more painful and funny.

jokes.text: You obsess about the Jamaican man across the hall. Oh, my god. I lost my job.
jokes.setup: Rosemary continues describing their similarities
jokes.punchline: Liz realizes the horrible accuracy and snaps back to reality
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with Liz's dawning horror. The specific 'Jamaican man' detail is perfectly weird.

jokes.text: Help me, Liz Lemon! You're my only hope!
jokes.setup: Rosemary desperately calls out to leaving Liz
jokes.punchline: Uses Princess Leia's line from Star Wars
jokes.explanation: Decent pop culture reference but fairly obvious choice. Shows Rosemary's desperation and cultural references.

jokes.text: What, are you kidding me? This is a billion dollar company! Demerits?
jokes.setup: Pete discovering the page-off situation
jokes.punchline: Adult incredulity at childish corporate policies
jokes.explanation: Good reality check moment. Pete represents audience incredulity at corporate absurdity.

jokes.text: Never go with a hippie to a second location.
jokes.setup: Liz reflects on her experience with Rosemary
jokes.punchline: Delivers perfectly crafted life advice based on her ordeal
jokes.explanation: Brilliant line that became iconic. Perfect economy, universal applicability, and emerges naturally from the story. This is 30 Rock at its absolute best.

jokes.text: You make enough money, you can pay people to look at you naked.
jokes.setup: Jack responds to Rosemary's theory about women becoming obsolete
jokes.punchline: Cynical but practical solution involving wealth and power
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack wisdom - cynical but oddly empowering. The economy and darkness make it memorable.

jokes.text: Oh, and by the way, ge has a problem with the dog penis sketch.
jokes.setup: Jack ends their conversation on a business note
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions corporate objection to absurd content
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows how corporate concerns override artistic vision. Jack's casual delivery is perfect.

jokes.text: Hey, what about cat penises?
jokes.setup: Liz learns about GE's objection to dog penises
jokes.punchline: Immediately suggests alternative animal genitalia
jokes.explanation: Good ending showing Liz's practical problem-solving applied to absurd corporate constraints. Shows her adaptation to the system.
